headlines.title: Oakland Councilmember Rowena Brown assaulted and robbed, her office says
headlines.description: Rowena Brown, Oakland’s at-large city councilmember, was assaulted and robbed “by an acquaintance she had been supporting through a difficult time,” her office said Thursday. The assault and robbery happened Saturday evening in Oakland, according to Brown’s office. She sought medical treatment and is recovering. “She remains focused on her recovery and on continuing to serve the Oakland community,” Brown’s office said in a statement. Brown’s office said the suspect was taken into custody. “We are grateful to the Oakland Police Department for its swift response, for taking the suspect into custody, and for its continued investigation of the incident,” Brown’s office said in a statement. “We ask that Councilmember Brown’s privacy be respected as she recovers.” Additional information wasn’t immediately available.

headlines.title: OpenAI says rogue AI models broke free from human control. Some see it as a ‘warning shot'
headlines.description: It is the kind of development once seen only in science fiction: An artificial intelligence system, trained to probe for digital vulnerabilities, breaks free of human control and acts on its own to hack another company. The attack announced this week by OpenAI, which blamed rogue AI models, underscored the blistering growth in the technology’s capabilities. For many, it also added urgency to questions about whether and how it can be prevented from causing mayhem on a bigger scale, with more serious consequences. In what OpenAI called an “unprecedented” episode, the company said its advanced AI models used stolen credentials to break into the servers of an AI startup. It started in what was supposed to be a “highly isolated” testing environment, with reduced guardrails, before the AI agent found its way onto the internet. But the disclosure brought a told-you-so moment for researchers who have called for a slowdown of AI development and warned for years that the technology could pose existential risks to humanity. In its wake, experts have called for improved testing by the AI companies and more dialogue between the U.S. and China to come up with shared solutions. “I think we’ve got to take this as a warning shot to not make them smarter, and that probably is going to require global collaboration,” said Nate Soares, co-author of the 2025 book “If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies.” The hack could pressure companies to improve containment If a model can decide to do something unethical, illegal or harmful on its own, what — if anything — can humans do to prevent it from doing so? OpenAI said it had tasked the AI models involved with pursuing “advanced exploitation using complex attack paths” to test cyber capabilities, but the technology went to unexpected lengths. It apparently decided on its own to target Hugging Face, a well-known AI development hub and marketplace, to obtain information it needed to carry out a task. Zahra Timsah, the co-founder and CEO of governance platform i-GENTIC AI, said she expects the incident to increase pressure on OpenAI and its competitors to complete rigorous testing and explore containment more thoroughly before AI systems are made accessible to the public. Monitoring an agent’s behavior after the fact, as OpenAI is now doing with its investigation, is no longer enough, she said. “It’s like having a seat belt, air bags, brakes, everything in the car. It should be there before the car starts driving,” Timsah said. The disclosure comes amid heightened concerns about the cybersecurity capabilities of powerful models. In June, President Donald Trump signed an executive order creating a framework for the federal government to vet the national security risks of the most advanced AI systems for up to a month before their public release. Other experts see the event as a sign of AI’s growing pains Some experts say the hack is part of the trial and error that comes with improving cybersecurity capabilities and is no cause for panic. “We’ve been dealing with people creating cybersecurity attacks for as long as the internet has existed. And one of the interesting properties of these language models is that the same capabilities that make them able to perform cybersecurity attacks also allow them to do cybersecurity threat analysis and make cybersecurity defenses,” said John Thickstun, an assistant professor of computer science at Cornell University who studies methods that control the behavior of AI models. The disclosure has raised skepticism from those who say it advantages OpenAI to make its technology seem scarier. Given that humans at OpenAI had decided to turn off some safeguards for the test, some have argued the outcome should not have been terribly surprising. Thickstun noted the disclosure plays into the need of OpenAI, a startup working toward a Wall Street debut, to raise money. “The story that they’ve been consistently telling over the lifetime of this company is a story about how dangerous their models are, which their investors read as a story of how powerful their language models are,” he said. The disclosure renews calls for more regulation The hack renewed calls in some corners for increased regulations and oversight of AI companies. U.S. Rep. Greg Casar, a Texas Democrat, wrote on social media: “We need regular mandatory independent safety testing and oversight, mandatory disclosure of security incidents, and international cooperation to keep people safe from absolute disaster.” Soares, director of the Machine Intelligence Research Institute, said the U.S. will need to open talks with its biggest AI competitor, China, something he thinks is not as outlandish as it might have seemed even a year ago. China’s leader Xi Jinping warned at a conference just last week of the need to keep AI from evading human control. And after an early aversion to regulating AI, Trump’s administration has grown more restrictive at reining in cybersecurity risks. “A lot can change when the national security community starts to notice that they have a serious threat,” Soares said. “Will this wake them up? Hopefully. I’m not sure. If this doesn’t, maybe the next incident will.” AI pioneer Yoshua Bengio said on social media the episode is deeply concerning and should serve as a “wake-up call.” “Continuing on the current trajectory of AI development will likely lead to an increase in concrete cases of autonomous cyberattacks as well as other high-risk incidents of misaligned and dangerous AI behavior,” said Bengio, a professor at the University of Montreal. “We urgently need to take action to prevent these situations, rather than attempting to clean up the damage after the fact.”
headlines.source: Barbara Ortutay | The Associated Press, Kaitlyn Huamani | The Associated Press and Matt O'brien | The Associated Press

headlines.title: Crews extinguish large fire at San Jose recycling center
headlines.description: A large overnight fire at a recycling center in San Jose has been extinguished. The fire was reported at about 1:39 a.m. Thursday in the 200 block of Leo Avenue, near Monterey Road. Firefighters arrived to find multiple large piles of burning debris outside an abandoned warehouse at the recycling center. Crews used aerial master streams and hand lines to knock down the flames by 2:09 a.m. The fire was brought under control at 2:37 a.m. Smoke from the fire was visible from Highway 87. All units have since cleared the scene. No injuries were reported, and the cause of the fire remains under investigation.

headlines.title: Republican Hilton tells Californians they can hate Trump but still vote for him in governor's race
headlines.description: Republican Steve Hilton launched his general election campaign for California governor Wednesday with a gambit to win over left-leaning voters who dominate the state: Even if they loathe President Donald Trump, he said, they should consider voting for him. During an event in Los Angeles, against the backdrop of a stinking, scorched food warehouse he described as a symbol of the state’s political incompetence, Hilton rolled out a list of campaign promises clearly aimed at residents buckling under California’s notoriously high cost of living. He vowed to bring the price of gasoline down to $3 a gallon, about half the current cost, cut residents’ utility bills in half, now among the nation’s highest, and eliminate state income taxes on the first $150,000 in earnings. He blamed the state’s problems — from homelessness to low student test scores — squarely on Democrats, who hold every statewide office and supermajorities in the Legislature. He called Democrats a “rotten regime that has abandoned the working class.” In his campaign remarks and ads he took out in leading newspapers throughout the state, Hilton addressed his relationship with Trump, who has endorsed him in the race. “This is not about who you hate in Washington. It’s about what we love about California and what we can do to make California once again the best place anywhere in the country to start and raise a family, to start and run a business,” he said before a small group of supporters. “That’s what this election is about.” Hilton’s maneuver is meant to create a sliver of distance from Trump without breaking from him. It’s a tacit nod to the political math in California, one of the nation’s most solidly Democratic states. Registered Republicans are outnumbered nearly 2-to-1 by Democratic voters, and a GOP candidate hasn’t won a statewide election since 2006. Trump is widely unpopular in California outside his loyal conservative base. In 2016, Trump lost to Democrat Hillary Clinton by 30 points; in 2020, he lost to Joe Biden by 29 points; and in 2024, he lost to Kamala Harris by 20 points. California is regarded as the home of the so-called Trump resistance, where Democrats have effectively used Trump as a foil in campaigns to motivate voters. Last year, Gov. Gavin Newsom essentially nationalized the state’s fight over Proposition 50, which recast U.S. House districts to favor Democrats, by framing it as a confrontation with Trump. The measure notched a 29-point win. The campaign of Hilton’s Democratic opponent, former U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Xavier Becerra, scoffed at the notion of Hilton being a change agent. “Californians already know who Steve Hilton is: Trump’s puppet in California. His campaign is a promise to run our state the way Trump runs the country and we’re not buying it,” campaign spokesman Jonathan Underland said in a statement. As for Hilton’s pitch that voters who dislike Trump should feel free to consider him in November, Underland said, “Steve Hilton cheers on a president whose illegal war has sent gas prices soaring and illegal tariffs have cost California families more than $2,500 this year alone. You can’t fix the affordability crisis while carrying water for the man causing it.” In a heavily Democratic state, Hilton’s campaign is hoping his appeal to Democrats and independents will “give voters a permission slip” to look past the letter next to a candidate’s name, even in a nation deeply divided by partisanship. “You do have a choice if you think that this state is going in the wrong direction,” he told supporters at a kickoff event in Boyle Heights, a Los Angeles neighborhood where residents have been complaining about the stench of rotting food, flies and rodents that followed a fire last month at a cold storage warehouse. He was accompanied by Gloria Romero, a former Democratic state lawmaker who switched parties in 2024 and is this year’s Republican nominee for lieutenant governor. She delivered her own address in both English and Spanish, with supporters holding “Hilton-Romero” signs in the background. Becerra, a former state lawmaker, congressman and state attorney general, is heavily favored to win the seat now held by Newsom, a likely 2028 Democratic presidential candidate who is barred by law from seeking a third term. Hilton and Becerra emerged from a chaotic primary election in June that had more than 50 candidates on the ballot. The race is unfolding at a time when California is facing multiple crises. Despite billions in spending, homelessness remains widespread, especially in larger cities. Multibillion-dollar budget gaps are projected into the future. Home prices remain out of reach for many residents. The home insurance market is in disarray, businesses and residents have been fleeing the state, and residents contend with some of the nation’s highest gas prices, taxes and utility bills. Making good on his campaign promises won’t be an easy task for Hilton, a former adviser to ex-United Kingdom Prime Minister David Cameron who has never held elective office. The governor doesn’t control the oil or utility markets, but Hilton says he will get rid of mandates, taxes and fees that drive those prices higher. He also would likely need the consent of a Legislature controlled by Democrats to change the state’s income tax structure. Hilton crowned his campaign kickoff with a 30-second TV ad underscoring the financial challenges for many families. “Life here has become impossible for anyone who isn’t already rich,” he says. “Let’s fix California.”
headlines.source: Michael R. Blood | The Associated Press

headlines.title: Woman found beaten to death near I-80 in Vallejo
headlines.description: Police in Vallejo are investigating the city’s seventh homicide this year after a woman was found beaten to death, the department said Wednesday. On Monday just before 2:30 p.m., officers were informed of a possible deceased person near Georgia and Steffan streets. Officers arrived and found an unresponsive woman near the landscaped interchange infield between the Interstate Highway 80 eastbound ramp and Steffan Street. Medical first responders declared the woman deceased. Detectives and the coroner’s office at the scene saw injuries on the woman and she was taken for a further examination by a medical examiner, police said. On Wednesday, the medical examiner determined she sustained blunt force trauma that led to her death. The woman’s identity is being withheld pending confirmation. Anyone with information regarding this case is urged to contact Detective Stephanie Diaz at 707-648-5430 or via email at Stephanie.Diaz@cityofvallejo.net, or Detective Zach Horton at 707-648-5425 or Zach.Horton@cityofvallejo.net. To remain anonymous, please call the tip line at 1-800-488-9383.

headlines.title: Bay Area couple continues month-long search for missing dog in Monterey County
headlines.description: A San Francisco couple is asking for the public’s help finding their dog after the pet went missing more than a month ago in Monterey County. Luca disappeared while in the care of a pet sitter near the Laguna Seca Golf Ranch on June 26. Since then, his owners have remained in the area, determined to bring him home. The couple is asking anyone who may have seen Luca to report sightings through their Nextdoor page or to the SPCA. More than a month after Luca’s disappearance, the owners say they remain hopeful. “We’re asking everyone, please keep your eyes peeled,” said Megan Kao, one of Luca’s owners. The couple is also offering a reward for Luca’s safe return.

headlines.title: DA clears San Jose officers in fatal shooting after child's killing
headlines.description: Santa Clara County prosecutors have ruled that two police officers acted lawfully when they fatally shot a Fremont man who charged at them with a knife moments after he killed his 9-year-old son in a San Jose park last summer. The Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office’s investigation found the officers reasonably believed they and others faced an imminent threat when Mateusz Dzierbun, 48, ran toward them with a knife raised over his head at Cataldi Park on July 13, 2025. “The officers were confronted with an extraordinarily dangerous and rapidly evolving situation,” the District Attorney’s report read. “The evidence before them indicated only two possibilities: either Dzierbun had already murdered his son, demonstrating his homicidal intent, or the child had been stabbed by Dzierbun and required immediate lifesaving aid.” According to the report, Dzierbun called 911 at 3:23 p.m. and initially claimed another man had stabbed his son before later telling the dispatcher that he had killed the child, saying, “They were going to torture him… I had to do it.” When Officers Eduardo Eyer and Kevin McAlindon arrived about eight minutes later, they found Dzierbun sitting on the grass about 50 feet from his son’s body while holding a knife. The officers repeatedly ordered him to drop the weapon so they could reach the injured child. Instead, Dzierbun told officers, “I am armed, and one of you guys is going to kill me today. I don’t want to traumatize you.” He later added, “Whoever killed someone before needs to be here.” As Officer McAlindon moved to retrieve a stun gun, Dzierbun warned, “If you get the stun bag, I’m going to get up and charge this guy,” according to the report. Body-worn camera footage reviewed by prosecutors showed Dzierbun then stood, raised the knife above his head and ran toward the officers. Sgt. Joel Casey and Officer Eyer opened fire, striking him 14 times. According to the report, Officer Eyer later said, “I need to save myself and my partners here… I wanted to save that kid.” When officers reached the child, they found he had already died from his injuries. The fatal stabbing and police shooting unfolded while a group nearby was taking pickleball lessons, according to the report. San Jose Police Chief Paul Joseph previously said investigators believed Dzierbun intended to die in a “suicide by cop.”

headlines.title: Copper wire theft incidents leave Morgan Hill streets in the dark
headlines.description: Morgan Hill police are investigating two copper wire theft incidents that left some streets in a neighborhood in the dark. Morgan hill police said the first theft happened Saturday and the second early Wednesday morning in the Community Park neighborhood. Police say the thefts caused tens of thousands of dollars in damage and repair costs. “We’ve been here 28 years. I can’t see how somebody could steal all that copper wiring up and down the block without the police or some witness seeing it,” Ernesto Lopez said. Copper wire theft has been an ongoing problem across the Bay Area. Last year, thieves stole copper from lighting along a stretch of interstate 680 in San Jose, leaving parts of the freeway dark. Earlier this year in Richmond, hundreds of streetlights also went dark after copper was stolen. “Copper theft has been an issue for quite a number of years because it’s easy access, and it they can take it to a number of reciclers and get cash, and as we all know metals have done nothing but increase in value,” Law Enforcement Analyst and former SJPD Officer Michael Leininger said. Leininger says preventing these crimes is challenging. “You’re generally relying on recyclers to follow the law and report suspicious persons or incidents,” he said. “Law enforcement, PG&E, the light companies, all need to take a step back and look at how these wires are contained and perhaps come up with a different containment system.” Leininger says surveillance cameras can be a key investigative tool as detectives look for any video that may have captured the thieves. Anyone with information is asked to contact Morgan Hill police.

headlines.title: Oakland Unified School District last to announce state law to limit phone usage
headlines.description: A new California law will require all public schools to have students limit phone usage during the day. Oakland Unified School District is the last to announce this change. For the upcoming academic year, texting, calling and scrolling will be prohibited. Students will be required to store their phones in locked spaces from the beginning to end of the school day. Elementary students will store them in classroom lock boxes. Middle and high school students will lock theirs inside pouches they’ll carry—but won’t be able to open. “It’s going to take some getting used to, there is going to be some growing pains but we are prepared for it,” OUSD spokesperson John Sasaki The state’s goal is to increase face to face interaction, prevent cyberbullying and have less distractions during class. The policy is required for all California public schools, however each district will implement it differently. Some districts, such as Oakland, will ban phone use during passing periods and lunch rather than just class time. Researchers tracked more than 43,000 schools nationwide and found locking phones away cut student phone use, but didn’t lead to immediate improvements in test scores, attendance, or cyberbullying. Co-author of the report Thomas Dee says more research is needed to evaluate long-term benefits. “It may be that phones were never fundamentally the problem or alternatively, it may be that phone bans do not go far enough. We hear from some schools that things like online bullying have simply shifted from phones to laptops and other devices,” he said. Several Bay Area districts that have already gone phone-free say they’ve seen students more engaged in class and a decline in harassment. OUSD’s policy will go into effect on Aug. 10.

headlines.title: South San Jose post office remains closed a year after arson
headlines.description: The Almaden Valley post office in South San Jose remains closed a year after an arson left part of the building destroyed. The arson occurred in July 2025 when Richard Tillman, the brother of former NFL player Pat Tillman, crashed a car with flammable logs into the post office. Based on the building’s appearance today, the post office appears to be a long way out from reopening. Residents and officials have voiced frustration with how long the repair process is taking. “Seems unreasonable that they couldn’t fix the damage in this amount of time,” resident Bob Burriss said. Congressman Jimmy Panetta said he pressured the United States Postal Service to speed up repairs and finally received a completion target date. First it was the spring of 2027, but that has since been pushed to August 2027. In a statement, the USPS said the facility will reopen when the necessary work is completed and it is deemed safe for staff and customers. Residents in the area currently have to travel about six miles to the closest USPS location in Willow Glen.

headlines.title: Ruby Hill families return home after Little Fire forces them to evacuate
headlines.description: The fire line came withing yards of homes in Ruby Hill on Tuesday, prompting evacuation orders. Families in the area had to make some tough decisions as they didn’t know if they were leaving home for a night or potentially for the last time. “All these things you said probably would never happen? Well guess what? In a second, they could happen,” Ruby Hill resident Wendy Mcgee said. That second came Tuesday afternoon, as the Little Fire raced across the hillsides east of Pleasanton, forcing thousands of people from their homes. Just minutes earlier, McGee’s Ring camera captured a police officer at her front door… warning her the flames were closing in. “He said, ‘Yeah, you’re one of the first houses and you’re probably closest to the fire’,” McGee said. “When I turned my back and looked out the back window, I could see the red flames on the hill and I panicked.” As the fire pushed toward the neighborhood, families had to decide what was worth taking and what they had to leave behind. “I came home and I grabbed all my videos, my scrapbooks, my computer, pretty much everything I knew was irreplaceable,” Ruby Hill resident Mokie Marchi said. All this, as they were being told repeatedly to leave. “Our phones went off blaring… evacuate, evacuate, evacuate,” Marchi said. Neighbors checked on one another, some turned on sprinklers and others simply hoped they’d be coming home. By Wednesday, most families were able to return and find their homes still standing. “It was nothing short of a miracle and I looked at it and thought ‘how much do we worry about the little things?’” McGee said. For many of these families, that perspective may be the one thing they’ll carry with them long after this fire is out. While evacuation orders have now been lifted or downgraded, the neighbors I spoke with say they’re already making emergency plans and creating grab-and-go lists because they now know firsthand how quickly everything can change.

headlines.title: Preliminary 2.7 magnitude earthquake strikes near San Leandro
headlines.description: A preliminary 2.7 magnitude earthquake struck near San Leandro, according to the United States Geological Survey. The quake hit at 5:52 p.m., and was centered 1.1 miles north-northeast of San Leandro, the USGS said. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. BAY AREA QUAKE CENTRAL Bay Area Quake Tracker, the latest earthquake stories, extensive quake prep checklists, videos and many other disaster preparedness resources all in one place: NBCBAYAREA.COM/QUAKES
